articles.headline: India's Green Hydrogen Mission Accelerates with New Investment Pledges
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) announced significant private sector investment pledges for green hydrogen production and related infrastructure.
• This push aims to establish India as a global hub for green hydrogen production and export, contributing to energy security and decarbonization.
• Focus areas include developing electrolyser manufacturing capabilities, creating demand for green hydrogen in sectors like refining and fertilizer, and establishing green hydrogen corridors.
• The mission targets a production capacity of at least 5 Million Metric Tonnes (MMT) per annum by 2030, with an associated renewable energy capacity addition of about 125 GW.
• This initiative is crucial for India to meet its Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) under the Paris Agreement and achieve net-zero emissions by 2070.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation
Energy
Infrastructure: Energy
Economic development
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission launched in 2023.
Target: 5 MMT green hydrogen production by 2030.
Associated renewable energy capacity addition: ~125 GW.
Mission outlay: Rs. 19,744 crore.
Nodal Ministry: MNRE.
SIGHT program: Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic significance of the Green Hydrogen Mission for India's energy security and geopolitical standing.
Analyze the challenges and opportunities in establishing a robust green hydrogen ecosystem in India.
Evaluate the role of green hydrogen in achieving India's climate targets and fostering sustainable economic growth.
Examine the potential for India to become a global leader in green hydrogen production and export, including technological and policy enablers.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of green hydrogen production facility with wind turbines and solar panels in the background.
articles.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, making up roughly 75% of all normal matter.

articles.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Easing Inflation Concerns
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) concluded its latest review, deciding to keep the benchmark repo rate unchanged.
• The decision comes as retail inflation shows signs of moderation, though global uncertainties persist, warranting a cautious approach.
• RBI reiterated its commitment to aligning inflation with the target while supporting growth, maintaining an 'withdrawal of accommodation' stance.
• Experts anticipate a gradual shift towards rate cuts later in the fiscal year, contingent on sustained disinflationary trends and stable economic growth.
• Liquidity management measures were also discussed, with the RBI affirming its flexibility to manage systemic liquidity conditions effectively.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning
mobilization of resources
growth
development and employment
Government Budgeting
prelimsPoints: MPC consists of 6 members (3 RBI, 3 GoI nominees).
Repo rate is the rate at which RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Inflation target is 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
Withdrawal of accommodation stance implies focus on bringing inflation down to target.
Liquidity operations by RBI include LAF, MSF, OMOs.
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale behind RBI's 'withdrawal of accommodation' stance and its implications for economic growth and inflation.
Discuss the challenges faced by the MPC in balancing inflation control with supporting economic growth in the current global and domestic scenario.
Evaluate the effectiveness of monetary policy tools in addressing supply-side inflation and external shocks.
Examine the interplay between fiscal policy and monetary policy in India, particularly in managing government debt and inflation.
articles.imageDescription: Reserve Bank of India building in Mumbai.
articles.trivia: The Reserve Bank of India was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

articles.headline: India-US Strategic Dialogue Focuses on Tech, Defence, and Regional Stability
articles.explainer:
• The latest round of the India-US Strategic Dialogue concluded with renewed commitments to deepen cooperation across various sectors.
• Key discussions revolved around critical and emerging technologies, defence industrial collaboration, and strengthening supply chain resilience.
• Both nations emphasized their shared vision for a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific, addressing regional security challenges and promoting multilateralism.
• Agreements were reached on enhancing collaboration in space, artificial intelligence, and cybersecurity, reflecting the growing strategic convergence.
• The dialogue also touched upon climate change cooperation, global health initiatives, and people-to-people ties.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
Indian Diaspora
prelimsPoints: India and US are members of Quad.
IPEF launched in 2022, includes India and US.
CET initiative aims to boost cooperation in AI, quantum computing, semiconductors.
Foundational agreements in defence: LEMOA, COMCASA, BECA.
Annual 2+2 Ministerial Dialogue is a key forum.
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolving contours of the India-US strategic partnership, particularly in the context of global geopolitical shifts and challenges.
Discuss the significance of critical and emerging technologies in shaping the future of India-US cooperation and its implications for India's technological sovereignty.
Evaluate India's role in the Indo-Pacific strategy of the US and how it aligns with India's own strategic interests in the region.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for deepening defence industrial collaboration between India and the US, including 'Make in India' initiatives.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and United States displayed together.
articles.trivia: The first official state visit by an Indian Prime Minister to the U.S. was by Jawaharlal Nehru in 1949.

articles.headline: Government Finalizes Rules for Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) announced the finalization of rules for implementing the Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act), 2023.
• The rules provide clarity on the roles and responsibilities of Data Fiduciaries and Data Processors, consent mechanisms, and the rights of Data Principals.
• A key highlight is the establishment of the Data Protection Board of India, which will adjudicate disputes and enforce compliance with the Act.
• The framework aims to balance the need for data innovation with robust protection for individual privacy, aligning India with global data protection standards.
• Industry stakeholders are now preparing for the comprehensive rollout and compliance with the new regulations, which are expected to significantly impact digital businesses.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies
keyTerms: Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act), 2023
prelimsPoints: DPDP Act, 2023, received presidential assent in August 2023.
Data Protection Board of India is an independent body.
Act applies to processing of digital personal data within India.
Significant Data Fiduciaries have enhanced obligations.
Penalty for non-compliance can go up to Rs. 250 crore.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, and its potential impact on individual privacy and the digital economy in India.
Discuss the role and powers of the Data Protection Board of India in enforcing data protection standards and addressing grievances.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for businesses in complying with the new data protection framework, especially for MSMEs and startups.
Compare India's data protection framework with international standards like GDPR, highlighting similarities and differences.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration representing digital data and privacy shield.
articles.trivia: The Supreme Court of India declared the Right to Privacy as a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Constitution in the Puttaswamy judgment (2017).

articles.headline: Government Launches New Schemes to Boost Climate Resilience in Agriculture
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare introduced a suite of new initiatives focused on making Indian agriculture more resilient to climate change impacts.
• These schemes integrate traditional knowledge with modern technology, promoting drought-resistant crops, water-efficient irrigation, and climate-smart farming practices.
• Special emphasis is placed on providing farmers with timely climate information, early warning systems, and access to climate-resilient seeds and livestock.
• The initiatives also aim to enhance farmer incomes by reducing crop losses due to extreme weather events and promoting diversified farming systems.
• Collaboration with research institutions and state governments is a cornerstone of these efforts to ensure widespread adoption and effectiveness.
gsSubTopics: Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country
different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage
transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints
e-technology in the aid of farmers
Climate Change
keyTerms: National Adaptation Fund for Climate Change (NAFCC)
prelimsPoints: National Innovations in Climate Resilient Agriculture (NICRA) is a flagship program.
Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchai Yojana (PMKSY) focuses on water use efficiency.
e-NAM platform aids farmers in marketing produce.
Soil Health Card Scheme promotes balanced fertilization.
ICAR plays a crucial role in agricultural research.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multifaceted impacts of climate change on Indian agriculture and the necessity for climate-resilient farming practices.
Discuss the efficacy of government schemes in promoting climate-smart agriculture and addressing the vulnerabilities of small and marginal farmers.
Evaluate the role of technology and traditional knowledge in building agricultural resilience against extreme weather events.
Suggest policy measures to further strengthen climate change adaptation and mitigation in India's agricultural sector.
articles.imageDescription: Farmers working in a field with modern irrigation systems under changing weather conditions.
articles.trivia: India is one of the world's largest agricultural producers, with agriculture contributing significantly to its GDP and employment.

articles.headline: ISRO's Shukrayaan-1 Venus Orbiter Mission Enters Critical Design Review Phase
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced that its ambitious Shukrayaan-1 mission to Venus has successfully entered its Critical Design Review (CDR) phase.
• This milestone signifies the completion of detailed design work and a thorough assessment of the mission's technical readiness and scientific objectives.
• Shukrayaan-1 aims to study Venus's atmosphere, surface, and subsurface, utilizing advanced instruments developed by Indian and international partners.
• The mission is expected to provide crucial insights into Venus's runaway greenhouse effect and its implications for planetary habitability.
• Launch is anticipated in the late 2020s, with a window of opportunity opening every 19 months for an optimal trajectory to Venus.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology
indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Space
prelimsPoints: Shukrayaan-1 is India's first mission to Venus.
Expected launch period is late 2020s.
Objective: study Venus's atmosphere, surface, subsurface.
Instruments include a synthetic aperture radar and a ground-penetrating radar.
Venus is often called Earth's 'sister planet' due to similar size and mass.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of planetary exploration missions like Shukrayaan-1 for advancing scientific knowledge and India's capabilities in space technology.
Analyze the scientific objectives of Shukrayaan-1 and how its findings could contribute to understanding planetary evolution and climate change on Earth.
Evaluate the role of international collaboration in ISRO's deep-space missions and its benefits for global scientific community.
Examine the challenges involved in designing and executing missions to Venus, considering its extreme environment.
articles.imageDescription: Artistic rendition of the Shukrayaan-1 spacecraft orbiting Venus.
articles.trivia: Venus rotates in the opposite direction to most other planets in our solar system, a phenomenon known as retrograde rotation.

articles.headline: National Logistics Policy Driving Efficiency and Cost Reduction, Says Commerce Ministry
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Commerce and Industry reported significant progress in the implementation of the National Logistics Policy (NLP), launched in 2022.
• The policy aims to reduce logistics costs as a percentage of GDP, which currently stands at 13-14%, to a global benchmark of 8%.
• Key achievements include improved multimodal connectivity, enhanced digital integration through platforms like ULIP (Unified Logistics Interface Platform), and streamlined regulatory processes.
• These efforts are contributing to increased competitiveness for Indian businesses, both domestically and internationally, by reducing transit times and operational costs.
• The government is also focusing on skill development in the logistics sector and promoting green logistics practices.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models
Growth and development
prelimsPoints: National Logistics Policy (NLP) launched in September 2022.
Goal: reduce logistics cost to 8% of GDP.
PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan is foundational for NLP.
ULIP integrates digital services across logistics ecosystem.
Multimodal connectivity includes road, rail, air, and waterways.
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and key pillars of the National Logistics Policy and its potential to transform India's logistics sector.
Discuss how improved logistics infrastructure and digital integration can enhance India's global competitiveness and boost exports.
Evaluate the challenges in achieving the target of reducing logistics costs and suggest measures to overcome them.
Examine the role of public-private partnerships and technology adoption in modernizing India's logistics ecosystem.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic illustrating various modes of transport (truck, train, ship, plane) integrated into a logistics network.
articles.trivia: The logistics sector in India is one of the largest employers, providing livelihoods to millions.

articles.headline: India Expands Wetland Conservation Efforts with Designation of New Ramsar Sites
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) announced the addition of several new sites to the list of Wetlands of International Importance (Ramsar Sites) in India.
• This expansion underscores India's commitment to conserving its rich wetland biodiversity and fulfilling its obligations under the Ramsar Convention.
• Wetlands play a crucial role in maintaining ecological balance, providing critical ecosystem services such as water purification, flood control, and habitat for diverse flora and fauna.
• The new designations will bring increased attention and resources for the scientific management and sustainable utilization of these vital ecosystems.
• Local communities and stakeholders will be actively involved in the conservation plans to ensure their long-term viability.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Biodiversity
prelimsPoints: Ramsar Convention signed in Ramsar, Iran, in 1971.
India joined the Ramsar Convention in 1982.
Chilika Lake was one of the first Ramsar sites in India.
Montreux Record is a register of Ramsar sites where changes in ecological character have occurred, are occurring, or are likely to occur.
Wetlands are transition zones between terrestrial and aquatic systems.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ecological and economic significance of wetlands and the threats they face in India.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the Ramsar Convention and national policies in promoting wetland conservation and management in India.
Analyze the challenges in integrating local communities into wetland conservation efforts and suggest strategies for inclusive management.
Examine the impact of climate change on wetland ecosystems and the role of wetlands in climate change mitigation and adaptation.
articles.imageDescription: A serene wetland landscape with migratory birds and diverse vegetation.
articles.trivia: World Wetlands Day is celebrated annually on February 2nd, marking the date of the adoption of the Convention on Wetlands in 1971.

articles.headline: Report Highlights Need for Enhanced Financial Autonomy for Urban Local Bodies
articles.explainer:
• A recent report by a parliamentary standing committee emphasized the critical need for greater financial autonomy and resource mobilization for Urban Local Bodies (ULBs).
• The report pointed out that ULBs often rely heavily on state and central grants, limiting their ability to undertake long-term planning and infrastructure development.
• Recommendations include empowering ULBs to levy and collect a wider range of taxes, rationalizing property tax mechanisms, and exploring innovative financing models like municipal bonds.
• Strengthening ULBs is crucial for effective urban governance, service delivery, and achieving sustainable development goals in rapidly urbanizing India.
• The committee also stressed the importance of capacity building for ULB officials and promoting transparency in financial management.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States
issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure
Devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein
prelimsPoints: 74th Constitutional Amendment Act (1992) gave constitutional status to ULBs.
12th Schedule lists 18 functions of municipalities.
State Finance Commissions recommend devolution of funds to ULBs.
Municipal bonds are debt instruments issued by ULBs to raise capital.
Property tax is a major source of own revenue for ULBs.
mainsPoints: Analyze the constitutional provisions and practical challenges related to the financial autonomy of Urban Local Bodies in India.
Discuss the implications of inadequate financial resources for urban infrastructure development and service delivery.
Evaluate the various mechanisms for enhancing revenue generation for ULBs, including property tax reforms and municipal bonds.
Suggest comprehensive reforms to strengthen urban governance and empower ULBs to become engines of sustainable urban development.
articles.imageDescription: A cityscape with a municipal building in the foreground, representing urban governance.
articles.trivia: The first municipal corporation in India was set up in Madras (now Chennai) in 1688.

articles.headline: E-Courts Project Phase III: Enhancing Digital Justice Delivery Across India
articles.explainer:
• The Department of Justice, in collaboration with the Supreme Court e-Committee, announced the successful completion of the initial rollout of Phase III of the e-Courts Project.
• This phase focuses on leveraging advanced technologies like Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) for case management, judicial data analytics, and virtual court proceedings.
• The objective is to further enhance accessibility to justice, improve efficiency in judicial processes, and reduce pendency of cases across all levels of the judiciary.
• Key features include integrated case information systems, e-filing portals, and provisions for digital hearings, especially beneficial for remote areas and during emergencies.
• Training programs for judicial officers and court staff are also being intensified to ensure seamless adoption of the new digital tools.
gsSubTopics: Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary
Ministries and Departments of the Government
e-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations and potential
prelimsPoints: e-Courts Project is a Mission Mode Project.
Phase I and II were focused on computerization of courts.
Phase III integrates AI/ML for advanced functionalities.
Supreme Court e-Committee oversees the project.
Objective is to provide citizen-centric services.
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolution and impact of the e-Courts Project in enhancing justice delivery and reducing judicial pendency in India.
Discuss the potential of Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in transforming judicial processes and improving access to justice.
Evaluate the challenges associated with the implementation of digital justice systems, including digital divide and cybersecurity concerns.
Suggest measures to ensure equitable access to e-justice for all sections of society, including those in rural and remote areas.
articles.imageDescription: A virtual court hearing in progress on a computer screen.
articles.trivia: India has one of the largest judicial systems in the world, with over 19,000 courts nationwide.

articles.headline: India Reiterates Support for Global Minimum Corporate Tax at G20 Finance Meeting
articles.explainer:
• India reaffirmed its commitment to the global minimum corporate tax framework during the recent G20 Finance Ministers and Central Bank Governors meeting.
• The framework, championed by the OECD, aims to ensure that multinational corporations pay a minimum effective tax rate of 15% on their profits, regardless of where they operate.
• India sees this as a crucial step towards curbing tax evasion, preventing profit shifting, and ensuring a fairer international tax system.
• Discussions focused on the timeline for implementation, addressing concerns of developing nations, and ensuring administrative simplicity.
• The move is expected to significantly increase tax revenues for many countries and promote greater tax equity globally.
gsSubTopics: Government Budgeting
Indian Economy
Effect of liberalization on the economy
International institutions
prelimsPoints: Global minimum corporate tax rate proposed is 15%.
OECD/G20 Inclusive Framework on BEPS developed the framework.
Pillar One deals with reallocation of taxing rights.
Pillar Two introduces global minimum tax.
Objective is to address challenges of taxing digital economy.
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale behind the global minimum corporate tax and its potential impact on international tax regimes and corporate behavior.
Discuss the benefits and challenges for India in implementing the global minimum tax, considering its economic structure and investment climate.
Evaluate the role of G20 and OECD in shaping global economic governance and addressing issues like tax evasion and profit shifting.
Examine how the global minimum tax could affect India's tax revenue generation and its competitiveness as an investment destination.
articles.imageDescription: A world map with currency symbols, representing global taxation.
articles.trivia: The G20 was formed in 1999 in response to the Asian financial crisis, evolving into a key forum for international economic cooperation.

articles.headline: Coastal Security Enhanced with New Surveillance Technologies and Inter-Agency Coordination
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Defence and the Ministry of Home Affairs announced a series of measures to further bolster India's coastal security apparatus.
• These include the deployment of advanced surveillance technologies, such as integrated coastal surveillance systems and unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), along vulnerable stretches.
• Emphasis is being placed on strengthening inter-agency coordination between the Indian Navy, Coast Guard, Marine Police, and customs authorities for real-time intelligence sharing and joint operations.
• The initiatives aim to counter threats like maritime terrorism, smuggling, illegal fishing, and infiltration, safeguarding India's vast coastline and island territories.
• Community participation through coastal vigilance committees is also being promoted to create a multi-layered security framework.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas
Linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
keyTerms: National Command Control Communication and Intelligence (NC3I) Network
prelimsPoints: India has a coastline of over 7,500 km.
Indian Coast Guard is the principal agency for coastal security in EEZ.
Marine Police operates in territorial waters.
IMAC at Gurugram integrates coastal surveillance data.
26/11 Mumbai attacks led to significant coastal security reforms.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multifaceted threats to India's coastal security and the evolution of its coastal defence strategy since 2008.
Discuss the challenges in achieving seamless coordination among various agencies involved in coastal security and suggest measures for improvement.
Evaluate the effectiveness of technological advancements and community involvement in strengthening coastal vigilance and deterrence.
Examine the geopolitical implications of maritime security challenges in the Indian Ocean Region for India's national interests.
articles.imageDescription: A patrol boat of the Indian Coast Guard on a surveillance mission along the coastline.
articles.trivia: India has 13 major ports and 200 non-major ports along its coastline.

articles.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission Expands Services, Focus on Telemedicine and Digital Records
articles.explainer:
• The National Health Authority (NHA) announced the expansion of services under the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM), with a renewed focus on telemedicine and digital health records.
• The mission aims to create a seamless online platform for healthcare, allowing patients to access medical services remotely and securely manage their health data.
• Key initiatives include promoting the adoption of Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA IDs) among citizens and integrating more healthcare providers into the digital ecosystem.
• This expansion is critical for improving healthcare accessibility, especially in rural and underserved areas, and for building a more resilient health system.
• Efforts are also underway to standardize digital health formats and ensure interoperability across various healthcare platforms.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health
Human Resources
Government policies and interventions
prelimsPoints: ABDM launched in September 2021.
NHA is the implementing agency for ABDM.
ABHA ID is a 14-digit unique health identification number.
Telemedicine guidelines were released by MCI (now NMC).
PM-JAY is the health insurance component of Ayushman Bharat.
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and key components of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission and its potential to revolutionize healthcare delivery in India.
Discuss the benefits and challenges of integrating telemedicine and digital health records into India's diverse healthcare landscape.
Evaluate the role of technology in improving healthcare access, affordability, and quality, especially for marginalized populations.
Examine the ethical and privacy concerns associated with digital health records and the measures required to safeguard patient data.
articles.imageDescription: A doctor conducting a telemedicine consultation on a tablet with a patient.
articles.trivia: India's National Medical Commission (NMC) replaced the Medical Council of India (MCI) in 2020.

articles.headline: India Aims for Greater Role in Global Semiconductor Supply Chain with New Incentives
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) announced new incentives and policy adjustments to attract more investment in India's semiconductor ecosystem.
• The goal is to establish India as a significant player in the global semiconductor manufacturing and design supply chain, reducing reliance on imports.
• Focus areas include semiconductor fabrication units (fabs), display fabs, and design-linked incentive (DLI) schemes for chip design companies.
• This strategic push is critical for national security, economic growth, and supporting the 'Digital India' and 'Make in India' initiatives.
• The government is collaborating with academic institutions to build a skilled workforce necessary for the advanced semiconductor industry.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning
mobilization of resources
growth
development and employment
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: India Semiconductor Mission (ISM) is the nodal agency for this initiative.
PLI scheme for large-scale electronics manufacturing includes semiconductors.
DLI scheme supports chip design startups and MSMEs.
Semiconductors are crucial for almost all electronic devices.
Taiwan, South Korea, and the US are major players in global semiconductor manufacturing.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of the semiconductor industry for India's economic growth, national security, and technological sovereignty.
Discuss the challenges India faces in establishing a robust semiconductor manufacturing ecosystem and the policy measures being undertaken to overcome them.
Evaluate the potential of India to become a significant hub for semiconductor design and manufacturing in the global supply chain.
Examine the geopolitical implications of the global semiconductor shortage and India's efforts to build supply chain resilience.
articles.imageDescription: A close-up of a silicon wafer with numerous microchips.
articles.trivia: The first integrated circuit (chip) was invented by Jack Kilby and Robert Noyce independently in 1958.

articles.headline: ASI Undertakes Major Restoration Work at Ancient Temples in Southern India
articles.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced an extensive restoration project for several ancient temple complexes across southern India.
• The multi-year initiative focuses on preserving the structural integrity and artistic heritage of these sites, which are often centuries old and face threats from weathering and human activity.
• Advanced conservation techniques, including laser cleaning and traditional material replication, are being employed to ensure authenticity and durability.
• This project aligns with India's commitment to protecting its cultural heritage and promoting tourism, which contributes to local economies.
• Collaboration with local communities and religious trusts is a key aspect to ensure sustainable management and public engagement.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
prelimsPoints: ASI founded in 1861 by Alexander Cunningham.
Responsible for archaeological research and conservation of cultural heritage.
Many South Indian temples are examples of Dravidian architecture.
UNESCO World Heritage Sites in South India include Hampi, Mahabalipuram, Great Living Chola Temples.
Epigraphy is the study of inscriptions.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges involved in conserving and restoring ancient architectural marvels in India, considering climate, pollution, and human intervention.
Analyze the role of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) in protecting India's cultural heritage and promoting archaeological research.
Evaluate the economic and cultural significance of heritage tourism and how such restoration projects contribute to it.
Examine the ethical considerations in heritage conservation, balancing preservation with access and contemporary use.
articles.imageDescription: An ancient South Indian temple gopuram undergoing restoration work.
articles.trivia: The Brihadeeswarar Temple in Thanjavur, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, was built by Raja Raja Chola I in the 11th century.

articles.headline: Report Highlights Gaps in Implementation of Forest Rights Act, 2006
articles.explainer:
• A new report by a civil society organization detailed significant gaps and challenges in the effective implementation of the Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006.
• The report pointed to low rates of individual and community forest rights (IFR and CFR) recognition, particularly in critical forest areas and tribal regions.
• Issues cited include bureaucratic hurdles, lack of awareness among tribal communities, resistance from forest departments, and insufficient institutional support.
• The FRA aims to recognize the rights of forest-dwelling Scheduled Tribes and other traditional forest dwellers over forest land and resources, crucial for their livelihoods and conservation efforts.
• Recommendations include simplifying application procedures, strengthening Gram Sabhas, and ensuring convergence of development schemes with FRA implementation.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections
keyTerms: Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, 2006
prelimsPoints: FRA enacted in 2006.
Recognizes rights over forest land and forest produce.
Gram Sabha is the nodal authority to initiate the process of determining rights.
IFR for cultivation and habitation, CFR for use of forest produce and management.
Ministry of Tribal Affairs is the nodal ministry for FRA implementation.
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and key provisions of the Forest Rights Act, 2006, and its significance for tribal communities and forest conservation.
Discuss the major challenges and bottlenecks in the effective implementation of FRA across different states in India.
Evaluate the impact of non-implementation or partial implementation of FRA on the livelihoods, cultural identity, and empowerment of forest-dwelling communities.
Suggest policy and administrative reforms to strengthen FRA implementation and ensure the recognition of rights for all eligible beneficiaries.
articles.imageDescription: A tribal community meeting in a forest village, discussing their rights.
articles.trivia: India has over 700 tribal communities, with the largest concentration in the central and northeastern regions.

articles.headline: India Pushes for Enhanced Connectivity with Central Asian Nations
articles.explainer:
• India intensified its diplomatic efforts to enhance trade and transit connectivity with Central Asian countries, emphasizing the International North-South Transport Corridor (INSTC).
• Discussions during a regional summit focused on overcoming logistical hurdles, developing new transport routes, and streamlining customs procedures.
• This push is part of India's 'Connect Central Asia' policy, aimed at strengthening economic, strategic, and cultural ties with the resource-rich region.
• The strategic importance lies in diversifying India's trade routes, accessing energy resources, and countering regional geopolitical influences.
• Collaboration in energy, digital payments, and capacity building for Central Asian economies were also key agenda points.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: INSTC is a 7,200-km multi-modal transport corridor linking India to Central Asia and Europe via Iran and Russia.
Chabahar Port in Iran is crucial for INSTC and India's access to Afghanistan and Central Asia.
Ashgabat Agreement (2018) is a multimodal transport agreement between Central Asian and Persian Gulf states.
Central Asian countries: Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, Uzbekistan.
India is a full member of SCO.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic and economic significance of Central Asia for India's foreign policy and energy security.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities in developing robust connectivity infrastructure between India and Central Asian nations, particularly through INSTC.
Evaluate India's 'Connect Central Asia' policy in the context of regional geopolitical dynamics and the influence of other major powers.
Examine how enhanced connectivity can boost trade, investment, and cultural exchanges between India and Central Asia.
articles.imageDescription: A map highlighting the International North-South Transport Corridor (INSTC) route.
articles.trivia: The Great Game was a political and diplomatic confrontation between the British and Russian Empires over Central Asia from 1813 to 1907.

articles.headline: NITI Aayog Proposes National AI Ethics and Governance Framework
articles.explainer:
• NITI Aayog released a discussion paper outlining a proposed National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence (AI) Ethics and Governance Framework.
• The framework aims to guide the responsible development and deployment of AI technologies in India, ensuring fairness, transparency, accountability, and safety.
• Key principles include promoting human-centric AI, addressing algorithmic bias, ensuring data privacy, and establishing mechanisms for redressal.
• This initiative is crucial for fostering public trust in AI while harnessing its transformative potential across sectors like healthcare, education, and agriculture.
• Stakeholder consultations are being initiated to gather feedback from industry, academia, civil society, and government bodies before finalization.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: NITI Aayog is a policy think tank of the Government of India.
AI applications include natural language processing, computer vision, machine learning.
Ethical concerns: bias, privacy, accountability, job displacement.
India's National Strategy for AI (2018) 'AI for All' vision.
Responsible AI principles are globally debated.
mainsPoints: Analyze the ethical challenges and societal implications arising from the rapid development and deployment of Artificial Intelligence technologies in India.
Discuss the key components of NITI Aayog's proposed AI Ethics and Governance Framework and its potential to ensure responsible AI development.
Evaluate the balance between fostering innovation in AI and establishing robust regulatory mechanisms to address its risks.
Examine the role of government, industry, and civil society in building a trustworthy and human-centric AI ecosystem in India.
articles.imageDescription: An abstract representation of AI, with human hands interacting with digital code and a brain icon.
articles.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956 at the Dartmouth Conference.

articles.headline: India Outlines Strategy to Improve Ranking in Global Hunger Index
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Women and Child Development presented a multi-pronged strategy to address malnutrition and improve India's performance in the Global Hunger Index (GHI).
• The strategy focuses on enhancing the reach and effectiveness of existing nutrition programs like Poshan Abhiyaan and the Mid-Day Meal Scheme.
• Key interventions include promoting dietary diversity, improving maternal and child health services, and strengthening food security measures, especially in vulnerable regions.
• Data-driven monitoring and community participation are emphasized to ensure targeted delivery and sustainable outcomes.
• The government aims to reduce child wasting, stunting, and mortality rates, which are key indicators used in the GHI calculation.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to poverty and hunger
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
prelimsPoints: GHI is published annually by Concern Worldwide and Welthungerhilfe.
Four indicators: Undernourishment, Child Wasting, Child Stunting, Child Mortality.
Poshan Abhiyaan (2018) aims to reduce malnutrition.
Mid-Day Meal Scheme provides cooked meals to school children.
Food Security Act, 2013, provides subsidized food grains.
mainsPoints: Analyze the causes and consequences of malnutrition in India, particularly among women and children, and its impact on human development.
Discuss the components and effectiveness of government initiatives like Poshan Abhiyaan and the Mid-Day Meal Scheme in addressing hunger and malnutrition.
Evaluate the methodology and relevance of the Global Hunger Index for India and its implications for policy formulation.
Suggest comprehensive strategies, including inter-sectoral convergence and community engagement, to achieve food and nutrition security in India.
articles.imageDescription: A plate of nutritious food, symbolizing efforts to combat hunger and malnutrition.
articles.trivia: India ranks among the top countries globally in terms of the number of children suffering from stunting and wasting.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Bench to Hear Review Petitions on Electoral Bond Scheme Verdict
articles.explainer:
• A Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court is scheduled to hear review petitions challenging its earlier verdict that struck down the Electoral Bond Scheme.
• The original verdict, delivered in February 2024, declared the scheme unconstitutional, citing violations of the right to information and free and fair elections.
• Petitioners seeking review argue that the judgment overlooked certain aspects related to donor privacy and the need to curb black money in political funding.
• The outcome of the review could have significant implications for political funding transparency and electoral reforms in India.
• Public interest in the case remains high, emphasizing the ongoing debate between donor anonymity and electoral transparency.
gsSubTopics: Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies
prelimsPoints: Electoral Bond Scheme introduced in 2017.
Anonymous donations to political parties.
Supreme Court struck down the scheme in February 2024.
SBI was the authorized bank for issuing electoral bonds.
Article 19(1)(a) guarantees freedom of speech and expression, including right to information.
mainsPoints: Analyze the constitutional arguments for and against the Electoral Bond Scheme, particularly concerning the right to information and donor privacy.
Discuss the implications of the Supreme Court's verdict on electoral bonds for political funding transparency and the integrity of democratic processes in India.
Evaluate the challenges in reforming political funding in India and suggest alternative mechanisms to ensure transparency while curbing illicit money.
Examine the role of the judiciary in upholding democratic principles and safeguarding fundamental rights in matters of electoral reforms.
articles.imageDescription: Supreme Court of India building with gavels in the foreground.
articles.trivia: The first general elections in independent India were held from October 1951 to February 1952.

highlights: India's Green Hydrogen Mission gains momentum with significant private investment pledges, aiming for 5 MMT production by 2030.
RBI maintains status quo on repo rate, citing easing inflation concerns but global uncertainties persist, signalling cautious approach.
India-US Strategic Dialogue reinforces cooperation in critical technologies, defence, and a free Indo-Pacific, deepening bilateral ties.
Government finalizes rules for Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, establishing Data Protection Board for robust privacy framework.
Supreme Court to hear review petitions on its verdict striking down the Electoral Bond Scheme, reigniting debate on political funding transparency.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the National Green Hydrogen Mission, consider the following statements:
1. The mission aims for a production capacity of at least 10 Million Metric Tonnes (MMT) per annum of green hydrogen by 2030.
2. The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) is the nodal ministry for its implementation.
3. The Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition (SIGHT) programme is a component of the mission.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect. The mission targets a production capacity of at least 5 Million Metric Tonnes (MMT) per annum by 2030. Statements 2 and 3 are correct. MNRE is the nodal ministry, and SIGHT is a key component.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following is NOT a component of the Global Hunger Index (GHI) calculation?
(a) Undernourishment
(b) Child Wasting
(c) Maternal Mortality Rate
(d) Child Mortality Rate
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Global Hunger Index (GHI) uses four indicators: Undernourishment, Child Wasting, Child Stunting, and Child Mortality Rate. Maternal Mortality Rate is not a direct component of the GHI calculation.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act), 2023:
1. It applies only to the processing of digital personal data outside the territory of India.
2. The Data Protection Board of India is established as an independent body to enforce its provisions.
3. The Act grants Data Principals the 'Right to be forgotten'.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect; the Act applies to the processing of digital personal data within India and also to processing outside India if it is for offering goods or services to Data Principals in India. Statement 2 is correct; the Data Protection Board of India is an independent body. Statement 3 is correct; the Act includes the 'Right to be forgotten' as a right of Data Principals.

prelimsMocks.question:
With respect to India's coastal security, which of the following agencies/bodies are involved?
1. Indian Navy
2. Indian Coast Guard
3. Marine Police
4. Information Management and Analysis Centre (IMAC)
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: All the listed agencies/bodies are involved in India's coastal security. The Indian Navy is responsible for overall maritime security, the Indian Coast Guard for coastal security in the EEZ, Marine Police for territorial waters, and IMAC for intelligence integration and analysis.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements about the International North-South Transport Corridor (INSTC) is/are correct?
1. It is a 7,200-km multimodal transport corridor.
2. It connects India to Central Asia and Europe via Iran and Russia.
3. Chabahar Port in Iran is a crucial component of INSTC.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three statements are correct. INSTC is indeed a 7,200-km multimodal corridor, designed to connect India to Central Asia and Europe through Iran and Russia, with Chabahar Port being a vital part of this connectivity project.

mainsMocks.question: Critically analyze the strategic significance of India's National Green Hydrogen Mission. Discuss the challenges and opportunities in establishing India as a global hub for green hydrogen production and export.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment; Infrastructure: Energy; Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the National Green Hydrogen Mission and its objectives. Discuss its strategic importance for energy security, decarbonization, and economic growth. Detail the opportunities (e.g., domestic demand, export potential, technological advancements, job creation). Analyze the challenges (e.g., high production costs, infrastructure development, technology scaling, water availability, policy consistency). Conclude with a forward-looking perspective on India's potential.

mainsMocks.question: The Supreme Court's verdict on the Electoral Bond Scheme has reignited the debate on political funding transparency. Discuss the constitutional and democratic implications of anonymous political donations and suggest alternative mechanisms to ensure transparency while curbing illicit money in elections.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act; Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary; Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
mainsMocks.approach: Begin by explaining the Electoral Bond Scheme and the Supreme Court's rationale for striking it down (Right to Information, free and fair elections). Discuss the arguments for and against anonymous donations (transparency vs. donor privacy, black money). Analyze the implications for democracy, accountability, and level playing field. Suggest comprehensive electoral reforms, including caps on donations, state funding, digital transparency, and stricter enforcement, to enhance transparency and curb black money.

mainsMocks.question: Despite the Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006, challenges persist in recognizing the rights of forest-dwelling communities. Analyze the major hurdles in its effective implementation and evaluate its impact on the livelihoods and empowerment of tribal communities. Suggest measures for strengthening its implementation.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections; Issues relating to poverty and hunger.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the FRA, 2006, and its objectives. Detail the various types of rights it seeks to recognize (IFR, CFR). Discuss major implementation challenges such as bureaucratic delays, lack of awareness, resistance from forest departments, and inadequate institutional capacity. Evaluate the impact on tribal livelihoods, cultural identity, and conservation efforts. Conclude by suggesting measures like simplifying procedures, capacity building, inter-departmental coordination, and strengthening Gram Sabhas for effective implementation.

quickBytes.text: India's target for green hydrogen production is 5 MMT per annum by 2030 under the National Green Hydrogen Mission.
The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) of RBI has six members.
The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, establishes the Data Protection Board of India.
Shukrayaan-1 is ISRO's first planned mission to Venus.
The National Logistics Policy aims to reduce logistics costs to 8% of GDP.
India joined the Ramsar Convention in 1982 for wetland conservation.
The 74th Constitutional Amendment Act gave constitutional status to Urban Local Bodies (ULBs).
The Global Minimum Corporate Tax rate proposed by OECD/G20 is 15%.
The Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006, recognizes rights of forest-dwelling Scheduled Tribes and other traditional forest dwellers.
The International North-South Transport Corridor (INSTC) is a 7,200-km multimodal corridor.

GS1.headline: ASI Undertakes Major Restoration Work at Ancient Temples in Southern India
GS1.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced an extensive restoration project for several ancient temple complexes across southern India.
• The multi-year initiative focuses on preserving the structural integrity and artistic heritage of these sites, which are often centuries old and face threats from weathering and human activity.
• Advanced conservation techniques, including laser cleaning and traditional material replication, are being employed to ensure authenticity and durability.
• This project aligns with India's commitment to protecting its cultural heritage and promoting tourism, which contributes to local economies.
• Collaboration with local communities and religious trusts is a key aspect to ensure sustainable management and public engagement.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
prelimsPoints: ASI founded in 1861 by Alexander Cunningham.
Responsible for archaeological research and conservation of cultural heritage.
Many South Indian temples are examples of Dravidian architecture.
UNESCO World Heritage Sites in South India include Hampi, Mahabalipuram, Great Living Chola Temples.
Epigraphy is the study of inscriptions.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges involved in conserving and restoring ancient architectural marvels in India, considering climate, pollution, and human intervention.
Analyze the role of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) in protecting India's cultural heritage and promoting archaeological research.
Evaluate the economic and cultural significance of heritage tourism and how such restoration projects contribute to it.
Examine the ethical considerations in heritage conservation, balancing preservation with access and contemporary use.
GS1.imageDescription: An ancient South Indian temple gopuram undergoing restoration work.
GS1.trivia: The Brihadeeswarar Temple in Thanjavur, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, was built by Raja Raja Chola I in the 11th century.

GS2.headline: India-US Strategic Dialogue Focuses on Tech, Defence, and Regional Stability
GS2.explainer:
• The latest round of the India-US Strategic Dialogue concluded with renewed commitments to deepen cooperation across various sectors.
• Key discussions revolved around critical and emerging technologies, defence industrial collaboration, and strengthening supply chain resilience.
• Both nations emphasized their shared vision for a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific, addressing regional security challenges and promoting multilateralism.
• Agreements were reached on enhancing collaboration in space, artificial intelligence, and cybersecurity, reflecting the growing strategic convergence.
• The dialogue also touched upon climate change cooperation, global health initiatives, and people-to-people ties.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
Indian Diaspora
prelimsPoints: India and US are members of Quad.
IPEF launched in 2022, includes India and US.
CET initiative aims to boost cooperation in AI, quantum computing, semiconductors.
Foundational agreements in defence: LEMOA, COMCASA, BECA.
Annual 2+2 Ministerial Dialogue is a key forum.
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolving contours of the India-US strategic partnership, particularly in the context of global geopolitical shifts and challenges.
Discuss the significance of critical and emerging technologies in shaping the future of India-US cooperation and its implications for India's technological sovereignty.
Evaluate India's role in the Indo-Pacific strategy of the US and how it aligns with India's own strategic interests in the region.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for deepening defence industrial collaboration between India and the US, including 'Make in India' initiatives.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and United States displayed together.
GS2.trivia: The first official state visit by an Indian Prime Minister to the U.S. was by Jawaharlal Nehru in 1949.

GS2.headline: Government Finalizes Rules for Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) announced the finalization of rules for implementing the Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act), 2023.
• The rules provide clarity on the roles and responsibilities of Data Fiduciaries and Data Processors, consent mechanisms, and the rights of Data Principals.
• A key highlight is the establishment of the Data Protection Board of India, which will adjudicate disputes and enforce compliance with the Act.
• The framework aims to balance the need for data innovation with robust protection for individual privacy, aligning India with global data protection standards.
• Industry stakeholders are now preparing for the comprehensive rollout and compliance with the new regulations, which are expected to significantly impact digital businesses.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies
keyTerms: Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act), 2023
prelimsPoints: DPDP Act, 2023, received presidential assent in August 2023.
Data Protection Board of India is an independent body.
Act applies to processing of digital personal data within India.
Significant Data Fiduciaries have enhanced obligations.
Penalty for non-compliance can go up to Rs. 250 crore.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, and its potential impact on individual privacy and the digital economy in India.
Discuss the role and powers of the Data Protection Board of India in enforcing data protection standards and addressing grievances.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for businesses in complying with the new data protection framework, especially for MSMEs and startups.
Compare India's data protection framework with international standards like GDPR, highlighting similarities and differences.
GS2.imageDescription: Illustration representing digital data and privacy shield.
GS2.trivia: The Supreme Court of India declared the Right to Privacy as a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Constitution in the Puttaswamy judgment (2017).

GS2.headline: Report Highlights Need for Enhanced Financial Autonomy for Urban Local Bodies
GS2.explainer:
• A recent report by a parliamentary standing committee emphasized the critical need for greater financial autonomy and resource mobilization for Urban Local Bodies (ULBs).
• The report pointed out that ULBs often rely heavily on state and central grants, limiting their ability to undertake long-term planning and infrastructure development.
• Recommendations include empowering ULBs to levy and collect a wider range of taxes, rationalizing property tax mechanisms, and exploring innovative financing models like municipal bonds.
• Strengthening ULBs is crucial for effective urban governance, service delivery, and achieving sustainable development goals in rapidly urbanizing India.
• The committee also stressed the importance of capacity building for ULB officials and promoting transparency in financial management.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States
issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure
Devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein
prelimsPoints: 74th Constitutional Amendment Act (1992) gave constitutional status to ULBs.
12th Schedule lists 18 functions of municipalities.
State Finance Commissions recommend devolution of funds to ULBs.
Municipal bonds are debt instruments issued by ULBs to raise capital.
Property tax is a major source of own revenue for ULBs.
mainsPoints: Analyze the constitutional provisions and practical challenges related to the financial autonomy of Urban Local Bodies in India.
Discuss the implications of inadequate financial resources for urban infrastructure development and service delivery.
Evaluate the various mechanisms for enhancing revenue generation for ULBs, including property tax reforms and municipal bonds.
Suggest comprehensive reforms to strengthen urban governance and empower ULBs to become engines of sustainable urban development.
GS2.imageDescription: A cityscape with a municipal building in the foreground, representing urban governance.
GS2.trivia: The first municipal corporation in India was set up in Madras (now Chennai) in 1688.

GS2.headline: E-Courts Project Phase III: Enhancing Digital Justice Delivery Across India
GS2.explainer:
• The Department of Justice, in collaboration with the Supreme Court e-Committee, announced the successful completion of the initial rollout of Phase III of the e-Courts Project.
• This phase focuses on leveraging advanced technologies like Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) for case management, judicial data analytics, and virtual court proceedings.
• The objective is to further enhance accessibility to justice, improve efficiency in judicial processes, and reduce pendency of cases across all levels of the judiciary.
• Key features include integrated case information systems, e-filing portals, and provisions for digital hearings, especially beneficial for remote areas and during emergencies.
• Training programs for judicial officers and court staff are also being intensified to ensure seamless adoption of the new digital tools.
gsSubTopics: Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary
Ministries and Departments of the Government
e-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations and potential
prelimsPoints: e-Courts Project is a Mission Mode Project.
Phase I and II were focused on computerization of courts.
Phase III integrates AI/ML for advanced functionalities.
Supreme Court e-Committee oversees the project.
Objective is to provide citizen-centric services.
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolution and impact of the e-Courts Project in enhancing justice delivery and reducing judicial pendency in India.
Discuss the potential of Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in transforming judicial processes and improving access to justice.
Evaluate the challenges associated with the implementation of digital justice systems, including digital divide and cybersecurity concerns.
Suggest measures to ensure equitable access to e-justice for all sections of society, including those in rural and remote areas.
GS2.imageDescription: A virtual court hearing in progress on a computer screen.
GS2.trivia: India has one of the largest judicial systems in the world, with over 19,000 courts nationwide.

GS2.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission Expands Services, Focus on Telemedicine and Digital Records
GS2.explainer:
• The National Health Authority (NHA) announced the expansion of services under the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM), with a renewed focus on telemedicine and digital health records.
• The mission aims to create a seamless online platform for healthcare, allowing patients to access medical services remotely and securely manage their health data.
• Key initiatives include promoting the adoption of Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA IDs) among citizens and integrating more healthcare providers into the digital ecosystem.
• This expansion is critical for improving healthcare accessibility, especially in rural and underserved areas, and for building a more resilient health system.
• Efforts are also underway to standardize digital health formats and ensure interoperability across various healthcare platforms.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health
Human Resources
Government policies and interventions
prelimsPoints: ABDM launched in September 2021.
NHA is the implementing agency for ABDM.
ABHA ID is a 14-digit unique health identification number.
Telemedicine guidelines were released by MCI (now NMC).
PM-JAY is the health insurance component of Ayushman Bharat.
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and key components of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission and its potential to revolutionize healthcare delivery in India.
Discuss the benefits and challenges of integrating telemedicine and digital health records into India's diverse healthcare landscape.
Evaluate the role of technology in improving healthcare access, affordability, and quality, especially for marginalized populations.
Examine the ethical and privacy concerns associated with digital health records and the measures required to safeguard patient data.
GS2.imageDescription: A doctor conducting a telemedicine consultation on a tablet with a patient.
GS2.trivia: India's National Medical Commission (NMC) replaced the Medical Council of India (MCI) in 2020.

GS2.headline: Report Highlights Gaps in Implementation of Forest Rights Act, 2006
GS2.explainer:
• A new report by a civil society organization detailed significant gaps and challenges in the effective implementation of the Forest Rights Act (FRA), 2006.
• The report pointed to low rates of individual and community forest rights (IFR and CFR) recognition, particularly in critical forest areas and tribal regions.
• Issues cited include bureaucratic hurdles, lack of awareness among tribal communities, resistance from forest departments, and insufficient institutional support.
• The FRA aims to recognize the rights of forest-dwelling Scheduled Tribes and other traditional forest dwellers over forest land and resources, crucial for their livelihoods and conservation efforts.
• Recommendations include simplifying application procedures, strengthening Gram Sabhas, and ensuring convergence of development schemes with FRA implementation.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections
keyTerms: Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, 2006
prelimsPoints: FRA enacted in 2006.
Recognizes rights over forest land and forest produce.
Gram Sabha is the nodal authority to initiate the process of determining rights.
IFR for cultivation and habitation, CFR for use of forest produce and management.
Ministry of Tribal Affairs is the nodal ministry for FRA implementation.
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and key provisions of the Forest Rights Act, 2006, and its significance for tribal communities and forest conservation.
Discuss the major challenges and bottlenecks in the effective implementation of FRA across different states in India.
Evaluate the impact of non-implementation or partial implementation of FRA on the livelihoods, cultural identity, and empowerment of forest-dwelling communities.
Suggest policy and administrative reforms to strengthen FRA implementation and ensure the recognition of rights for all eligible beneficiaries.
GS2.imageDescription: A tribal community meeting in a forest village, discussing their rights.
GS2.trivia: India has over 700 tribal communities, with the largest concentration in the central and northeastern regions.

GS2.headline: India Pushes for Enhanced Connectivity with Central Asian Nations
GS2.explainer:
• India intensified its diplomatic efforts to enhance trade and transit connectivity with Central Asian countries, emphasizing the International North-South Transport Corridor (INSTC).
• Discussions during a regional summit focused on overcoming logistical hurdles, developing new transport routes, and streamlining customs procedures.
• This push is part of India's 'Connect Central Asia' policy, aimed at strengthening economic, strategic, and cultural ties with the resource-rich region.
• The strategic importance lies in diversifying India's trade routes, accessing energy resources, and countering regional geopolitical influences.
• Collaboration in energy, digital payments, and capacity building for Central Asian economies were also key agenda points.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: INSTC is a 7,200-km multi-modal transport corridor linking India to Central Asia and Europe via Iran and Russia.
Chabahar Port in Iran is crucial for INSTC and India's access to Afghanistan and Central Asia.
Ashgabat Agreement (2018) is a multimodal transport agreement between Central Asian and Persian Gulf states.
Central Asian countries: Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, Uzbekistan.
India is a full member of SCO.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic and economic significance of Central Asia for India's foreign policy and energy security.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities in developing robust connectivity infrastructure between India and Central Asian nations, particularly through INSTC.
Evaluate India's 'Connect Central Asia' policy in the context of regional geopolitical dynamics and the influence of other major powers.
Examine how enhanced connectivity can boost trade, investment, and cultural exchanges between India and Central Asia.
GS2.imageDescription: A map highlighting the International North-South Transport Corridor (INSTC) route.
GS2.trivia: The Great Game was a political and diplomatic confrontation between the British and Russian Empires over Central Asia from 1813 to 1907.

GS2.headline: India Outlines Strategy to Improve Ranking in Global Hunger Index
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Women and Child Development presented a multi-pronged strategy to address malnutrition and improve India's performance in the Global Hunger Index (GHI).
• The strategy focuses on enhancing the reach and effectiveness of existing nutrition programs like Poshan Abhiyaan and the Mid-Day Meal Scheme.
• Key interventions include promoting dietary diversity, improving maternal and child health services, and strengthening food security measures, especially in vulnerable regions.
• Data-driven monitoring and community participation are emphasized to ensure targeted delivery and sustainable outcomes.
• The government aims to reduce child wasting, stunting, and mortality rates, which are key indicators used in the GHI calculation.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to poverty and hunger
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
prelimsPoints: GHI is published annually by Concern Worldwide and Welthungerhilfe.
Four indicators: Undernourishment, Child Wasting, Child Stunting, Child Mortality.
Poshan Abhiyaan (2018) aims to reduce malnutrition.
Mid-Day Meal Scheme provides cooked meals to school children.
Food Security Act, 2013, provides subsidized food grains.
mainsPoints: Analyze the causes and consequences of malnutrition in India, particularly among women and children, and its impact on human development.
Discuss the components and effectiveness of government initiatives like Poshan Abhiyaan and the Mid-Day Meal Scheme in addressing hunger and malnutrition.
Evaluate the methodology and relevance of the Global Hunger Index for India and its implications for policy formulation.
Suggest comprehensive strategies, including inter-sectoral convergence and community engagement, to achieve food and nutrition security in India.
GS2.imageDescription: A plate of nutritious food, symbolizing efforts to combat hunger and malnutrition.
GS2.trivia: India ranks among the top countries globally in terms of the number of children suffering from stunting and wasting.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Bench to Hear Review Petitions on Electoral Bond Scheme Verdict
GS2.explainer:
• A Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court is scheduled to hear review petitions challenging its earlier verdict that struck down the Electoral Bond Scheme.
• The original verdict, delivered in February 2024, declared the scheme unconstitutional, citing violations of the right to information and free and fair elections.
• Petitioners seeking review argue that the judgment overlooked certain aspects related to donor privacy and the need to curb black money in political funding.
• The outcome of the review could have significant implications for political funding transparency and electoral reforms in India.
• Public interest in the case remains high, emphasizing the ongoing debate between donor anonymity and electoral transparency.
gsSubTopics: Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies
prelimsPoints: Electoral Bond Scheme introduced in 2017.
Anonymous donations to political parties.
Supreme Court struck down the scheme in February 2024.
SBI was the authorized bank for issuing electoral bonds.
Article 19(1)(a) guarantees freedom of speech and expression, including right to information.
mainsPoints: Analyze the constitutional arguments for and against the Electoral Bond Scheme, particularly concerning the right to information and donor privacy.
Discuss the implications of the Supreme Court's verdict on electoral bonds for political funding transparency and the integrity of democratic processes in India.
Evaluate the challenges in reforming political funding in India and suggest alternative mechanisms to ensure transparency while curbing illicit money.
Examine the role of the judiciary in upholding democratic principles and safeguarding fundamental rights in matters of electoral reforms.
GS2.imageDescription: Supreme Court of India building with gavels in the foreground.
GS2.trivia: The first general elections in independent India were held from October 1951 to February 1952.

GS3.headline: India's Green Hydrogen Mission Accelerates with New Investment Pledges
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) announced significant private sector investment pledges for green hydrogen production and related infrastructure.
• This push aims to establish India as a global hub for green hydrogen production and export, contributing to energy security and decarbonization.
• Focus areas include developing electrolyser manufacturing capabilities, creating demand for green hydrogen in sectors like refining and fertilizer, and establishing green hydrogen corridors.
• The mission targets a production capacity of at least 5 Million Metric Tonnes (MMT) per annum by 2030, with an associated renewable energy capacity addition of about 125 GW.
• This initiative is crucial for India to meet its Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) under the Paris Agreement and achieve net-zero emissions by 2070.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation
Energy
Infrastructure: Energy
Economic development
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission launched in 2023.
Target: 5 MMT green hydrogen production by 2030.
Associated renewable energy capacity addition: ~125 GW.
Mission outlay: Rs. 19,744 crore.
Nodal Ministry: MNRE.
SIGHT program: Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic significance of the Green Hydrogen Mission for India's energy security and geopolitical standing.
Analyze the challenges and opportunities in establishing a robust green hydrogen ecosystem in India.
Evaluate the role of green hydrogen in achieving India's climate targets and fostering sustainable economic growth.
Examine the potential for India to become a global leader in green hydrogen production and export, including technological and policy enablers.
GS3.imageDescription: Illustration of green hydrogen production facility with wind turbines and solar panels in the background.
GS3.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, making up roughly 75% of all normal matter.

GS3.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Easing Inflation Concerns
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) concluded its latest review, deciding to keep the benchmark repo rate unchanged.
• The decision comes as retail inflation shows signs of moderation, though global uncertainties persist, warranting a cautious approach.
• RBI reiterated its commitment to aligning inflation with the target while supporting growth, maintaining an 'withdrawal of accommodation' stance.
• Experts anticipate a gradual shift towards rate cuts later in the fiscal year, contingent on sustained disinflationary trends and stable economic growth.
• Liquidity management measures were also discussed, with the RBI affirming its flexibility to manage systemic liquidity conditions effectively.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning
mobilization of resources
growth
development and employment
Government Budgeting
prelimsPoints: MPC consists of 6 members (3 RBI, 3 GoI nominees).
Repo rate is the rate at which RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Inflation target is 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
Withdrawal of accommodation stance implies focus on bringing inflation down to target.
Liquidity operations by RBI include LAF, MSF, OMOs.
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale behind RBI's 'withdrawal of accommodation' stance and its implications for economic growth and inflation.
Discuss the challenges faced by the MPC in balancing inflation control with supporting economic growth in the current global and domestic scenario.
Evaluate the effectiveness of monetary policy tools in addressing supply-side inflation and external shocks.
Examine the interplay between fiscal policy and monetary policy in India, particularly in managing government debt and inflation.
GS3.imageDescription: Reserve Bank of India building in Mumbai.
GS3.trivia: The Reserve Bank of India was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

GS3.headline: Government Launches New Schemes to Boost Climate Resilience in Agriculture
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare introduced a suite of new initiatives focused on making Indian agriculture more resilient to climate change impacts.
• These schemes integrate traditional knowledge with modern technology, promoting drought-resistant crops, water-efficient irrigation, and climate-smart farming practices.
• Special emphasis is placed on providing farmers with timely climate information, early warning systems, and access to climate-resilient seeds and livestock.
• The initiatives also aim to enhance farmer incomes by reducing crop losses due to extreme weather events and promoting diversified farming systems.
• Collaboration with research institutions and state governments is a cornerstone of these efforts to ensure widespread adoption and effectiveness.
gsSubTopics: Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country
different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage
transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints
e-technology in the aid of farmers
Climate Change
keyTerms: National Adaptation Fund for Climate Change (NAFCC)
prelimsPoints: National Innovations in Climate Resilient Agriculture (NICRA) is a flagship program.
